does: This paper introduces a complete-oracle evaluation framework for assessing grounded generation systems, specifically targeting the limitation that existing reference-free faithfulness metrics measure only precision. The authors use Formula 1 telemetry and NOAA weather forecasts as domains where ground truth is deterministically derived and complete—meaning every relevant fact for each decision is known. They create multilingual benchmarks (English, Spanish, Portuguese) with 7,253 Formula 1 decision instances and evaluate frontier models, demonstrating that high-precision systems often achieve this by abstaining (saying little), covering less than half of relevant facts. They show that incorporating recall (coverage of relevant facts) through F1-score reorders system rankings fundamentally, and demonstrate that fine-tuning small language models on complete oracles substantially improves their coverage-aware performance.
connects: This work builds on growing literature examining faithfulness in neural generation systems, extending beyond earlier reference-based metrics that rely on human-written references or reference-free approaches that only verify consistency between generated and source claims. It responds to known limitations in faithfulness evaluation—particularly the precision-only blind spot noted in systems that reward abstention over informativeness. The paper connects to work on evaluating long-form generation, question-answering systems, and fact verification where precision-recall tradeoffs matter. It also engages with the challenge that most existing faithfulness benchmarks lack complete ground truth, making recall measurement impossible. The focus on complete oracles relates to work in information extraction and structured prediction where gold-standard annotations can be exhaustive.
enables: This framework enables future research to properly balance coverage and correctness in grounded generation, establishing complete-oracle domains as a rigorous evaluation standard for faithfulness work. The identification of precision-only evaluation as fundamentally misaligned with desired system behavior motivates development of new training objectives and model architectures that jointly optimize coverage and accuracy. The successful application to multiple domains (telemetry, weather) and languages suggests potential extension to other structured-fact domains where complete oracles could be constructed. The finding that fine-tuning on complete-oracle data improves performance points toward data-centric approaches for improving faithfulness. Future work can use this framework to benchmark and compare generation approaches, design better training signals that penalize both hallucination and omission, and potentially develop more efficient methods for obtaining or approximating complete oracles in domains where they are harder to derive.
